title: Lamb tagine with apricots
subtitle: Moroccan slow-braised lamb with dried apricots and almonds
excerpt: Lamb shoulder braised low and slow with saffron, ginger, and ras el hanout until falling-tender, then sweet dried apricots added for the final half hour. The honey-glazed almonds scattered over the top are not optional — they are the textural counterpoint to the soft meat and fruit. A Friday dish.
sourceNotes: The combination of meat, dried fruit, and nuts in Moroccan tagines derives from the medieval Arab-Andalusian culinary tradition in which sweet and savoury were not separated. Lamb with apricots is one of the most common configurations alongside lamb with prunes. The honey-glazed almond garnish is characteristic of the Fez tradition.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the braised lamb and sauce without the almonds. Add fresh almonds when reheating.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Better the next day. Reheat gently and add the almonds fresh.

content.text: Use bone-in shoulder pieces for this recipe — the bone adds flavour to the sauce and the collagen in the shoulder keeps the meat moist during the long braise. Boneless shoulder works but produces a drier result. The cooking time is 1 hour 45 minutes total; the first hour is the braise, the last 45 minutes is with the apricots added.

The sauce should reduce to a thick, glossy consistency that coats the meat by the end of cooking. If it remains thin, remove the lid for the last 15 minutes. The honey-glazed almonds need only 5 minutes to prepare and make a significant difference to the finished dish.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a heavy casserole over high heat. Season the {{lamb-shoulder}} with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper}}. Brown the lamb in batches for 3 to 4 minutes per side until well coloured, then set aside. Reduce the heat to medium. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 5 minutes. Add the {{garlic}}, {{ginger-root}}, {{ras-el-hanout}}, and {{cinnamon-ground}} and cook for 1 minute. Return the lamb to the pot. Pour in the saffron water and 400 ml of cold water. Bring to a simmer, cover, and cook over low heat for 1 hour until the lamb is beginning to yield.

Add the {{dried-apricots}} and 1 tablespoon of the {{honey}} to the pot. Stir, then cover and cook for a further 30 minutes until the lamb is fully tender and the apricots are soft. Meanwhile, toast the {{blanched-almonds}} in a dry pan over medium heat for 3 to 4 minutes, shaking frequently, until golden. Add the remaining tablespoon of {{honey}} and stir until the almonds are glazed. Remove from the heat. Taste the tagine and adjust salt. Serve over couscous or with khobz, scattered with the honey-glazed almonds.

items.symptom: Sauce too thin at the end
items.cause: Lid kept on throughout, preventing reduction
items.fix: Remove the lid for the last 15 to 20 minutes and increase the heat slightly to drive off excess liquid

items.cause: Added too early or cooked too long after adding
items.fix: Add the apricots only in the final 30 minutes — they should hold their shape and remain distinct in the finished dish

items.cause: Pieces too large or heat too high, causing the meat to seize
items.fix: Reduce to the lowest possible simmer and continue in 15-minute increments. Shoulder needs a sustained low heat to break down the collagen

content.text: This is one of the sweet tagines that define the Moroccan cooking of Fez and Marrakech, where medieval culinary traditions of combining meat with dried fruit and nuts have been maintained. It is a dish for occasions: Eid, Friday lunch, or a guest. In Moroccan homes the tagine pot sits over a charcoal brazier for the long slow cook; a covered casserole over the lowest gas flame produces the same result. Serve with couscous and harissa on the side.
